title: podman generated systemd pod units exit when in WantedBy=default.target
body:
### Issue Description

I am running a podman pod via systemd that has a bunch of other container systemd units bound to it and when I reboot the VM the the pod unit starts and stops the podman pod over and over again until the systemd unit enters a deactivated state when using `WantedBy=default.target`. Moving it to `WantedBy=multi-unit.target` it does the same thing but since it is later in the systemd boot order it eventually will start and stay running.  I assume that there is something that isn't ready during startup that is preventing it from staying running using `WantedBy=default.target`. 

If using `WantedBy=default.target` and you log in and see that the pod unit has not started, running `systemctl start mycool-pod.service` allows it to start without issues along with all of the units that are using `BindTo=mycool-pod.service`.

Journal entries when using `WantedBy=default.target`

```

```

Digging into this a bit more. I set `--log-level debug` on the `podman pod create` and `podman pod start` commands in my systemd unit and I got these logs. [podman-pod-start-debug.log](https://github.com/containers/podman/files/11973225/podman-pod-start-debug.log) I couldn't find anything obvious as to what might be the problem. 

Here is the systemd unit for the pod:

```
[Unit]
Description=Podman %n
Wants=network-online.target
After=network-online.target
RequiresMountsFor=%t/containers
Wants=app1.service app2.service app3.service app4.service app5.service app6.service app7.service app8.service app9.service app10.service app11.service app12.service app13.service app14.service app15.service app16.service app17.service 
Before=app1.service app2.service app3.service app4.service app5.service app6.service app7.service app8.service app9.service app10.service app11.service app12.service app13.service app14.service app15.service app16.service app17.service 

[Service]
Environment=PODMAN_SYSTEMD_UNIT=%n
Restart=on-failure
TimeoutStopSec=70
ExecStartPre=/bin/rm -f %t/%N.pid %t/%N.pod-id
ExecStartPre=/usr/bin/podman pod create \
  --infra-conmon-pidfile %t/%N.pid \
  --pod-id-file %t/%N.pod-id \
  --exit-policy=stop \
  --replace \
  --name mycool \
  --add-host=app1:127.0.0.1 \
  --add-host=app2:127.0.0.1 \
  --add-host=app3:127.0.0.1 \
  --add-host=app4:127.0.0.1 \
  --add-host=app5:127.0.0.1 \
  --add-host=app6:127.0.0.1 \
  --add-host=app7:127.0.0.1 \
  --add-host=app8:127.0.0.1 \
  --add-host=app9:127.0.0.1 \
  --add-host=app10:127.0.0.1 \
  --add-host=app11:127.0.0.1 \
  --add-host=app12:127.0.0.1 \
  --add-host=app13:127.0.0.1 \
  --add-host=app14:127.0.0.1 \
  --add-host=app15:127.0.0.1 \
  --add-host=app16:127.0.0.1 \
  --add-host=app17:127.0.0.1 \
  --add-host=app18:127.0.0.1 \
  --add-host=app19:127.0.0.1 \
  --add-host=app20:127.0.0.1 \
  --add-host=app21:127.0.0.1 \
  --add-host=app22:127.0.0.1 \
  --add-host=app23:127.0.0.1 \
  --add-host=app24:127.0.0.1 \
  --add-host=app25:127.0.0.1 \
  -p 80:3080 \
  -p 443:3443
ExecStart=/usr/bin/podman pod start --pod-id-file %t/%N.pod-id
ExecStop=/usr/bin/podman pod stop --ignore --pod-id-file %t/%N.pod-id -t 10
ExecStopPost=/usr/bin/podman pod rm --ignore -f --pod-id-file %t/%N.pod-id
PIDFile=%t/%N.pid
Type=forking

[Install]
WantedBy=default.target
```

In the end I worked around this bug by using `Restart=always` on the above unit so it just restarts over and over again so eventually it starts after restart. This was not an issue until I upgraded to Fedora CoreOS 38.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

See above.

### Describe the results you received

A pod that is not started.

### Describe the results you expected

A pod and it stays running when started via systemd.

comments.body: From the log it looks like the container application exits and not podman itself. Are there no logs from your application itself?

That is weird. I have logged in right away and observed all of my container applications inside the pod startup (there are 17 containers inside this pod) and then the pod shuts down and so they all stop. The pod shouldn't exit unless all of the containers do. I do have one service that is not stable so if that one starts up first and exits, I guess that would cause the pod to exit as well because `--exit-policy=stop` is set.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
@jdoss Are you still seeing an issue, and do you believe this needs to be fixed in Podman?

Oops, sorry about that not following up and closing this out. This was an issue with one of my other service's systemd units that depends on the podman pod unit that would start quickly and die (due to code issue inside that service) and I had `--exit-policy=stop` set on the pod. This would cause the pod to exit because there was no active containers in the pod. Moving things to `WantedBy=multi-unit.target` was just a red herring.
